summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PARENTING – Where the parties sought that final parenting orders be made by consent in terms contained within a signed Minute of Order provided to the court at the re-opened hearing – Where the court is satisfied those final orders sought are in the children’s best interests

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

HOGAN J:

In this matter (which has a very lengthy history) allegations of very significant family violence were initially raised, predominantly by the mother against the father, and in relation to his asserted conduct toward the children.
The evidence before the Court of a historical nature also includes that, some lengthy years ago now, the father made significant verbal threats to a former partner and attended a mental health clinic seeking help about voicing very concerning comments about that former partner and his own concerns about, at that time, his possible and feared conduct towards her.
Further, an issue in relation to the father's anger management was the subject of evidence, including evidence relating to circumstances and discussions between these parents during the course of their relationship, and prior to the parental separation.
The matter has also included evidence that the mother's care of the children has been compromised by her own functioning and use or abuse of alcohol, on occasion.
The evidence has included that, over time, the mother had reported that the children had made verbal comments, which included allegations of sexual and physical violence alleged to have been perpetrated by the father. She also, on the evidence, reported that the children's behaviours had been dysregulated at various times, either after spending time with the father or in anticipation of the same.
Each parent, in essence, has contended, at various times, that the other represents a risk of harm to their children – whether that harm is physical, emotional or psychological via conduct, or via failing to promote and support their children to have and enjoy a meaningful relationship with the other parent.
The long-standing litigation history means that these children were very young when it started. They will be, respectively, eleven and nine years of age this year.
A consequence of the long-standing litigation history and the ongoing associated involvement of the Independent Children's Lawyer has meant that, over time, whilst attended by periods of disruption, the children have had the opportunity to spend time with their father under supervision, initially, for a lengthy period of time. The father has shown and demonstrated through his continued attendance at that time, significant commitment and dedication to the children. He has demonstrated a desire to be a part of their lives and to ensure that their relationships with him are sustained and supported.
A further consequence of the litigation history and the ongoing involvement of the Independent Children's Lawyer has been that, over the last nine months or so (again, unfortunately, attended by a hiatus) the children have had the opportunity to spend unsupervised time with the father, including for overnight periods. At this stage, that has predominantly been for a single overnight, albeit that there has been a period of up to three consecutive nights that the children have spent time with their father and been cared for by him and in his household.
The consequence of the parenting orders sought by the consent of both parents – with each asserting, via that consent, that it represents something that is in their children's best interests – will mean that the children will have the opportunity to spend alternate weekend time with their father from Friday afternoon until Sunday afternoon, together with time for half of the Easter and Christmas school holidays each year, and for all of the June/July and September/October school holidays. They will otherwise continue to live with their mother.
The submissions made by the legal representatives on behalf of each parent and on behalf of the Independent Children's Lawyer echo, in essence, that the parents have, through child focus, arrived at a set of orders which each considers now to be in their children's best interests.
A further consequence of those orders is, of course, the terms of the Notation recorded on it.
Both parents record, by the orders that each have signed, their view that it is appropriate and in their children's best interests that they continue to share joint decision-making about major long-term issues relating to their children. Both parents, by affixing their signatures to the orders that they seek be made by consent, record their agreement that the terms of the same are in their children's best interests.
Subject to striking through paragraph 13 of the signed Minute of Order, I make orders in terms of the Minutes sought by the parents and endorsed by the Independent Children's Lawyer, because I am persuaded, particularly given the parental consent to the same, that such orders are in these children's best interests.
The original minute will be placed with the file.
